title: Will Stablecoins Become The Backbone Of A New Monetary Order?
gist: ARK frames Tether's USDT as a modern, technologically superior version of pre-1913 US free banking, argues Tether has become a major and strategically important buyer of US Treasury debt, and previews a shift toward inflation-indexed, hard-asset-backed stablecoins plus Tether's imminent USAT launch for developed markets.

themes.theme: USDT's Origin as an Arbitrage-Friction Fix

insights.claim: Tether was created in 2014 specifically to solve the problem that arbitrageurs could not move dollars between exchanges fast enough to exploit bitcoin price gaps, since bank wires took one to two days.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Explains USDT's original product-market fit as a settlement-speed arbitrage tool, a use case distinct from its later store-of-value role — useful for understanding why stablecoin demand is structurally sticky across very different use cases.
insights.quote: arbitrageurs trading bitcoin across exchanges could not move dollars—between and among banks, brokers, and countries—quickly or inexpensively enough to take advantage of arbitrage opportunities
insights.locator: How Did Stablecoins, Particularly USDT, Emerge?

insights.claim: After expanding from the Omni Layer to Ethereum, Tron, Solana, and Avalanche between 2017 and 2019 while cutting fees and improving speed, USDT became the most-traded crypto asset by volume in 2019, surpassing bitcoin itself.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Multi-chain distribution, not just reserve quality, was a primary driver of USDT's liquidity dominance — a template other issuers (and competitors) are still trying to replicate.
insights.locator: Between 2017 and 2019, Tether expanded beyond Omni

insights.claim: By the end of 2019 Tether had already disclosed reserves including A1/A2-rated commercial paper and signaled an intent to shift toward Treasuries and cash, years before the GENIUS Act made high-quality reserves a legal requirement.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Tether's voluntary pre-regulatory shift toward Treasuries suggests its balance sheet was already converging on what regulation later mandated, reducing the odds that GENIUS Act compliance forces a disruptive reserve overhaul.
insights.locator: By the end of 2019, as competitors proclaimed

insights.claim: In the two years ending March 2022, USDT's supply grew 25-fold from $3.3 billion to $80 billion, driven primarily by emerging-market demand rather than crypto speculation.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: This is the historical base rate for how fast stablecoin supply can scale when a macro shock (currency devaluation + capital controls) hits — a reference point for sizing future EM stablecoin adoption waves.
insights.locator: COVID catapulted USDT into a period of exceptional growth

insights.claim: In Venezuela, Lebanon, and Argentina between 2020 and 2023, USDT shifted from a crypto-arbitrage tool into a de facto savings account, payment method, and store of value for households facing sharp currency devaluation.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Confirms USDT's core moat is retail-level currency substitution in high-inflation economies, not trading convenience — the demand base least likely to churn away even if crypto speculation cools.
insights.locator: Between 2020 and 2023, people in Venezuela, Lebanon, Argentina

insights.claim: Pandemic lockdowns that cut off access to physical black-market dollars, combined with younger family members introducing older relatives to USDT, accelerated a generational shift toward holding digital dollars in emerging markets.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Suggests stablecoin adoption in EM has a durable demographic/behavioral component (intra-family tech transfer), not just a crisis-driven spike that reverses once conditions normalize.
insights.locator: With reduced access to black-market dollars

themes.theme: Tether's Current Scale and Sovereign-Debt Footprint

insights.claim: As of the article's writing, Tether had $187 billion in stablecoin supply and 60% market share versus Circle's USDC at $75 billion, with more than 450 million global users growing by roughly 30 million per quarter.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Quantifies the competitive gap between USDT and USDC — Tether's ~2.5x supply lead and faster user-growth rate is the baseline for handicapping any USDC/USAT catch-up narrative.
insights.locator: With $187 billion in supply and 60% market share

insights.claim: Because Tether's Treasury bill holdings now rival those held by some developed nations, it has become one of the largest and fastest-growing sources of demand for US government debt, giving the US government a strategic interest in Tether's continued stability.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: A non-obvious linkage: Tether's growth is now intertwined with US debt-financing needs, which raises the odds that US policymakers act to protect rather than crack down on Tether — a de facto policy backstop for the stablecoin.
insights.locator: The US government has developed a strategic interest in Tether

insights.claim: As of January 2026, Tether held more than $5 billion in surplus collateral (corporate bonds, gold, bitcoin, secured loans) above the value of USDT liabilities in circulation.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: A concrete solvency buffer investors can track quarter to quarter as a leading indicator of reserve-quality deterioration or improvement.
insights.locator: Including corporate bonds, gold, bitcoin, and secured loans

insights.claim: Arthur Laffer's framework argues stablecoins represent a more efficient revival of pre-1913 'free banking' rather than a repeat of risky 'wildcat banking,' because on-chain transparency, audits, liquid secondary markets, and higher-quality Treasury/cash collateral fix the failures that plagued 19th-century private note issuers.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Gives investors a historical lens for judging stablecoin regulation debates: the bull case rests on today's technology closing the specific gaps (branching bans, illiquid collateral, no clearinghouse) that caused 19th-century failures, not on free banking being inherently safe.
insights.locator: Stablecoins share some similarities with the currencies of that era

insights.claim: From 1776 to 1913, cumulative US inflation was zero even under a decentralized, privately-issued currency system, whereas since the Federal Reserve's creation in 1913 the Consumer Price Index has risen more than 30-fold.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: The article uses this contrast as its core contrarian evidence that centralized currency issuance, not private/competitive issuance, has been the historical driver of inflation — a talking point likely to recur in stablecoin regulatory debates.
insights.quote: Since 1913, the Fed has enabled inflation: the Consumer Price Index (CPI) has soared more than 30-fold.
insights.locator: Since 1913, the Fed has enabled inflation

insights.claim: Laffer attributes 19th-century US 'wildcat banking' failures not to free banking itself but to specific US restrictions — bans on bank branching and requirements to post risky state bonds as collateral — contrasting with Scotland (1716-1845) and Canada (1817-1914), whose free-banking systems saw low inflation and few failures thanks to competitive redemption and clearinghouses.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Reframes the standard 'free banking = wildcat banking = bad' narrative used by stablecoin critics, giving bulls a historical rebuttal grounded in specific policy design rather than the concept of private money itself.
insights.locator: In contrast, the US experience (1837–1861)

insights.claim: Even in the flawed pre-Civil War US free-banking era, 'busted bank notes' that could not be redeemed averaged under 2% losses, coincidentally matching the Federal Reserve's current 2% inflation target.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: A provocative data point bulls can use to argue that free banking's worst-case currency debasement was no worse than the Fed's own deliberate inflation target — undercutting the 'stablecoins are riskier than fiat' argument.
insights.locator: settled at less than 2% on average which, interestingly, is the Fed’s inflation target today

insights.claim: The Civil War-era National Banking Acts deliberately taxed non-federal-bond-backed currency out of existence to centralize note issuance, which later caused currency scarcity, deflation, and bank panics that were used to justify creating the Federal Reserve in 1913.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Positions the Fed's founding as a policy response to a problem (currency scarcity) that centralization itself created — a framework investors can apply when assessing whether current stablecoin regulation risks repeating the same overcorrection.
insights.locator: Without enough government bonds in existence to satisfy reserve requirements

themes.theme: Next-Generation Stablecoin Design: Inflation-Indexed and Hard-Asset-Backed

insights.claim: Laffer predicts that widespread stablecoin adoption in developed markets will require a more advanced design than a static dollar peg: a stablecoin pegged to the dollar that also appreciates with inflation, preserving purchasing power against goods and services.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Signals a coming product category — inflation-indexed stablecoins — that could compete with today's flat $1-peg tokens in developed markets; worth watching which issuer ships this first.
insights.locator: Dr. Laffer argues that a more advanced model is necessary

insights.claim: Tether has already introduced Alloy (AUSDT), a gold-backed stablecoin, and XAUT, a tokenized gold product, letting users stay long bitcoin or gold while transacting in a stable-value instrument that appreciates with its collateral and expands borrowing capacity.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Tether is already live-testing the hard-asset-backed, appreciating-stablecoin concept Laffer describes as necessary for developed markets, ahead of any formal regulatory push.
insights.locator: Already, Tether has introduced Alloy (AUSDT)

insights.claim: Paolo Ardoino believes that under the GENIUS Act framework, any stablecoin that passes yield directly to holders should be classified as a security subject to SEC scrutiny, which is why yield-bearing 'tokenized money market' products remain restricted to accredited and qualified investors.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Implies mass-market, yield-bearing stablecoins are unlikely to reach retail investors soon under current regulatory logic — a key constraint for anyone modeling stablecoin-as-savings-product growth.
insights.locator: Paolo Ardoino believes that any stablecoin passing yield directly to users

insights.claim: Sky's (formerly MakerDAO) USDS stablecoin diversifies its backing across five tiers of risk/return: other stablecoins, on-chain overcollateralized crypto lending, AAA corporate debt, short-duration Treasury bills, and OTC crypto lending to vetted counterparties.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Offers a concrete template for how a decentralized stablecoin issuer balances yield generation against liquidity and default risk — a benchmark for evaluating other DeFi-native stablecoin collateral structures.
insights.locator: Now, USDS is incorporating diversified collateral

insights.claim: Sky's Peg Stability Module allows arbitrageurs to swap USDC directly for USDS to defend the peg, while its sUSDS token pays savings yield sourced from borrower interest, tokenized money-market funds, Treasuries, and DeFi allocations, making USDS function simultaneously as a payment medium and a global savings instrument.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Illustrates a dual-purpose (payments + savings) stablecoin architecture that could be a model competitors copy to capture both transactional and yield-seeking demand.
insights.locator: To stabilize the peg further, Sky uses its Peg Stability Module

themes.theme: Emerging Use Cases and Tether's US Market Entry

insights.claim: Ardoino identifies commodity settlement as one of stablecoins' fastest-growing use cases; Tether began facilitating oil trades in 2025, creating a surge in USDT demand from global commodity markets.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: A largely overlooked demand driver outside retail/crypto circles — commodity traders adopting USDT for settlement speed/cost could be a durable, scalable source of stablecoin volume independent of crypto market cycles.
insights.locator: one of the fastest growing use cases is the settlement of commodity transactions

insights.claim: Ardoino distinguishes stablecoin stickiness by market type: in developed economies with existing digital-dollar rails like Venmo, Cash App, and Zelle, a stablecoin typically acts only as a temporary settlement layer before conversion back to local currency, whereas in emerging markets with unstable currencies USDT persists as a savings vehicle and stays in circulation.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Implies Tether's core competitive moat — sticky EM balances — will not automatically translate into the US market, meaning its planned developed-market product must compete on different terms (speed/cost of settlement, not currency substitution).
insights.locator: if not integrated into local economies, a stablecoin typically serves as a temporary settlement layer

insights.claim: Tether plans to launch USAT, a new stablecoin tailored specifically for developed markets, within the next few months of the article's February 2026 publication date, marking its formal entry into the US financial market.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: A concrete, dated catalyst to monitor: USAT's launch (expected roughly mid-2026) will test whether Tether can replicate its EM dominance in the far more competitive, already-banked US market.
insights.locator: Tether will be launching USAT, a new stablecoin tailored for developed markets

insights.claim: ARK Investment Management discloses that it may hold a financial interest in Tether through various strategies and vehicles it manages, and acknowledges an inherent bias toward positive expected results in its forecasts.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: A material conflict-of-interest disclosure: ARK's bullish framing of stablecoins and Tether specifically should be weighed against its own potential financial stake in the outcome.
insights.quote: ARK Investment Management, LLC (“ARK”) may hold a financial interest in Tether through various strategies and investment vehicles it manages.
insights.locator: Disclaimer
